learning_objectives: Define digital learning environments and their key components
Distinguish between LMS, LCMS, PLE, and MOOC platforms
Evaluate the affordances and constraints of different platform types
readings: Dabbagh, N. & Bannan-Ritland, B. - Online Learning Ch. 1-2
Siemens, G. - Learning Management Systems: The Wrong Place to Start Learning

lecture_notes.introduction: A digital learning environment is more than software. It is an ecosystem of tools, content, interactions, and assessment mechanisms that together create the conditions for learning. This session maps the landscape.
key_concepts: LMS: centralized course management (Canvas, Moodle, Blackboard)
LCMS: content creation and management systems
PLE: Personal Learning Environments as learner-constructed ecosystems
MOOC platforms: Coursera, edX, and the massification of learning
Affordance theory applied to digital learning tools
lecture_notes.demonstration: Platform comparison exercise: create a feature matrix comparing Canvas, Moodle, and Google Classroom for a specific teaching scenario
setup_requirements: Access to demo accounts on Canvas, Moodle, and Google Classroom

review.value: An LMS is institution-controlled and course-centric; a PLE is learner-controlled and spans courses, drawing in tools and resources the learner finds useful
A tool's affordances are the actions it makes possible; a discussion forum affords asynchronous dialogue but does not afford real-time brainstorming

learning_objectives: Apply the Community of Inquiry framework to online course design
Design discussion activities that promote higher-order thinking
Evaluate synchronous vs. asynchronous interaction strategies
readings: Garrison, D.R. - E-Learning in the 21st Century Ch. 2-3
Anderson, T. - Modes of Interaction in Distance Education (article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Interaction is the lifeblood of learning. In digital environments, interaction does not happen automatically — it must be deliberately designed. The Community of Inquiry framework provides a research-based foundation for this design work.
key_concepts: Community of Inquiry: teaching presence, social presence, cognitive presence
Anderson's interaction equivalency theorem
Discussion design: beyond 'post and reply twice'
Synchronous tools: video conferencing, live polling, breakout rooms
Asynchronous tools: forums, wikis, collaborative documents
lecture_notes.demonstration: Discussion redesign challenge: transform a low-level recall discussion prompt into a structured academic controversy that promotes cognitive presence

review.value: Teaching presence (design + facilitation), social presence (group cohesion + personal expression), cognitive presence (triggering event → exploration → integration → resolution)
Deep, meaningful interaction in at least one of three forms (student-content, student-instructor, student-student) can sustain learning even if the other two are minimal

learning_objectives: Design intuitive course navigation structures in an LMS
Apply UX principles to online course design
readings: Krug, S. - Don't Make Me Think (Ch. 1-3, adapted for LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Students who cannot find content cannot learn from it. Navigation design is not a cosmetic concern; it directly affects cognitive load, engagement, and course completion rates.
key_concepts: Information architecture for online courses
Consistent module structure: overview, content, activity, assessment
Quality Matters standards for course design
Reducing extraneous cognitive load through clear organization
Accessibility in navigation: screen readers, keyboard navigation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Course navigation audit: evaluate a sample online course against Quality Matters standards and produce an improvement plan
setup_requirements: Sample course in LMS (provided by instructor)

review.value: Each module should follow the same structure: introduction/overview, learning materials, practice activity, assessment. Consistency reduces navigation confusion and lets learners focus on content.
A peer review process with 8 general standards and 42 specific review standards for online course design quality assurance

learning_objectives: Distinguish between blended, hybrid, and HyFlex models
Apply evidence-based principles for blended course design
Design activities that leverage the strengths of both modalities
readings: Garrison, D.R. & Vaughan, N. - Blended Learning in Higher Education Ch. 1-3
U.S. Department of Education - Evaluation of Evidence-Based Practices in Online Learning (meta-analysis)

lecture_notes.introduction: Blended learning consistently outperforms both fully online and fully face-to-face instruction in meta-analyses. The key is designing intentional integration of modalities, not simply adding technology to a traditional course.
key_concepts: Blended (some online replacement of face-to-face), hybrid (roughly 50/50), HyFlex (simultaneous options)
The replacement model: what moves online and why
Flipped classroom as a blended design pattern
Designing for seamless transitions between modalities
Equity considerations: ensuring all students can access both modalities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Flipped lesson design: take a traditional lecture topic and redesign it as a flipped session with pre-class video, in-class active learning, and post-class reflection
setup_requirements: Sample traditional lecture outline for redesign

review.value: Students in blended conditions performed modestly better than those in face-to-face conditions; the advantage appears to come from increased time-on-task and active learning, not the technology itself
Students choose each session whether to attend in person, synchronously online, or asynchronously; maximizes flexibility but adds significant design and facilitation complexity

learning_objectives: Design authentic assessments for online and blended courses
Address academic integrity challenges in digital assessment
Implement rubrics and peer review in LMS platforms
readings: Palloff, R. & Pratt, K. - Assessing the Online Learner Ch. 3-5
Emerging research on proctoring, open-book exams, and portfolio assessment

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment in digital environments must be rethought, not merely transferred from face-to-face formats. Traditional proctored exams are poorly suited to online learning; authentic performance-based assessments align better with both the medium and deeper learning goals.
key_concepts: Authentic assessment: real-world tasks that demonstrate competence
E-portfolios as longitudinal assessment tools
Peer review: design, calibration, and reliability
Academic integrity: design-based solutions vs. surveillance
Rubric design for consistent online grading
lecture_notes.demonstration: Assessment conversion exercise: transform a traditional midterm exam into an authentic online assessment that addresses the same objectives while reducing integrity concerns

review.value: Instead of proctoring, design assessments where cheating is difficult by nature: unique prompts, application to personal contexts, process documentation, and oral defense components
Collections of learner work over time that demonstrate growth, reflection, and competence; they shift assessment from single-point-in-time to longitudinal evidence

learning_objectives: Identify actionable learning analytics available in LMS platforms
Use data to identify at-risk students and improve course design
Address ethical concerns around learning analytics and student data
readings: Siemens, G. & Long, P. - Penetrating the Fog: Analytics in Learning and Education
Slade, S. & Prinsloo, P. - Learning Analytics: Ethical Issues and Dilemmas

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital learning environments generate vast amounts of data. Learning analytics transforms this data into actionable insights for improving instruction, supporting learners, and making evidence-based design decisions.
key_concepts: Types of learning analytics: descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive
LMS analytics: login frequency, content access patterns, assessment performance
Early warning systems and intervention triggers
Course design improvement through analytics feedback loops
Ethics: privacy, consent, algorithmic bias, and the right to be forgotten
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analytics dashboard interpretation: given a set of LMS analytics data, identify three at-risk students and three course design improvements

review.value: Models that use early-semester behavior (logins, assignment submission patterns, discussion participation) to flag students at risk of failure, enabling proactive intervention
Students may not consent to data collection; predictive models can encode biases; labeling students 'at risk' can become a self-fulfilling prophecy if not handled carefully

quizzes.topic: Digital Learning Environments and Online Interaction

questions.question: Using the Community of Inquiry framework, explain why a discussion forum with the prompt 'Share your thoughts on Chapter 3' is unlikely to produce meaningful cognitive presence. Redesign the prompt to reach the Integration phase.
questions.answer: The prompt only triggers the first phase (Triggering Event) and invites surface-level sharing rather than deep engagement. It provides no structure for exploration, integration, or resolution. Redesigned prompt: 'The author argues that blended learning outperforms face-to-face instruction because of increased time-on-task, not the technology itself. Find a counter-example from your experience or the research literature, and construct an argument for or against this claim. Respond to at least one classmate whose position differs from yours by identifying the strongest point in their argument and the weakest point.' This redesign promotes exploration (find evidence), integration (construct an argument), and resolution (evaluate peer perspectives).

rubric.key: Correctly diagnoses why original prompt fails using CoI
Redesigned prompt targets Integration or Resolution phase
Explains how redesign promotes cognitive presence

questions.question: What is Anderson's interaction equivalency theorem? Explain one practical implication for designing an asynchronous online course with limited instructor availability.
questions.answer: Anderson's theorem states that deep, meaningful learning can be sustained as long as at least one of the three forms of interaction (student-content, student-instructor, student-student) is at a high level. If instructor availability is limited (reducing student-instructor interaction), the designer should compensate by increasing student-content interaction (rich, interactive, well-designed content with embedded practice) and/or student-student interaction (structured peer discussion, collaborative projects, peer review).

rubric.key: Identifies reduced instructor interaction as the constraint
Proposes specific compensation strategy with justification

questions.question: Describe two design-based strategies for maintaining academic integrity in online assessments that do not rely on proctoring software. Explain why each strategy works.
questions.answer: Strategy 1: Authentic, personalized assessments — require students to apply concepts to their own workplace, community, or personal experience. Since each response is unique to the student's context, copying is impractical. This works because the assessment measures application, not recall. Strategy 2: Process-based assessment — require submission of drafts, outlines, annotated bibliographies, and reflections throughout the project. This creates a documented trail of original work. It works because fabricating an entire process is far more difficult than copying a final product.

rubric.key: First strategy clearly described with rationale
Second strategy clearly described with rationale
Both strategies are genuinely design-based, not surveillance-based

questions.question: An LMS dashboard shows that 80% of students access video lectures but only 35% complete the follow-up practice activities. What does this data suggest, and what course design change would you recommend?
questions.answer: The data suggests a disconnect between passive content consumption and active engagement. Students may be watching videos without deep processing, or the practice activities may have barriers (unclear instructions, too difficult, not perceived as valuable, or not connected to grades). Recommended change: embed low-stakes formative assessment questions within or immediately after each video (using tools like Kaltura or H5P) so that active retrieval practice is integrated into the viewing experience rather than requiring a separate step. This reduces friction and applies the testing effect to improve retention.

rubric.key: Accurate interpretation of the data pattern
Identifies plausible causes for the gap
Specific, actionable design recommendation with learning science rationale

major_assignments.title: Digital Learning Environment Design Project
major_assignments.description: Design a complete 4-week online or blended course module using an LMS platform (Canvas, Moodle, or Google Classroom). The module must include a welcome/orientation page, 4 content modules with consistent structure, at least 2 interactive learning activities, a discussion forum with structured prompts, and a summative assessment with rubric. Apply Community of Inquiry principles throughout.
objectives: Build a functional course module in an LMS platform
Apply Community of Inquiry framework to promote all three presences
Design consistent, accessible navigation and module structure
Create assessments aligned to learning objectives
deliverables: Functional LMS course module (shared access or screencast walkthrough)
Design rationale document (1,500 words) explaining CoI framework application
Navigation map/site map of the course structure

criteria: Intuitive navigation and logical content sequence
Teaching presence evident in design and facilitation cues
Social presence supported through discussion and collaboration
Cognitive presence promoted through structured inquiry activities
Assessments aligned to stated learning objectives
Mix of formative and summative assessment
Justified design decisions with reference to research
Research-based interaction design using CoI framework
Reflective design rationale with scholarly support

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on digital learning environment concepts and frameworks

participation.description: Online discussion participation, peer reviews, and collaborative activities

midterm_examination.description: Covers platforms, interaction frameworks, course design, and blended learning

final_project.description: LMS course module implementation with pilot testing and revision

course_policies.attendance: This blended course requires attendance at all in-person sessions and timely completion of all online activities. More than 2 missed in-person sessions or 3 late online submissions will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 5% per day for up to 5 days. The LMS design project has firm deadlines due to peer review scheduling.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review and collaborative discussion are integral course components. All design work and written submissions must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Original work is required. Properly attribute all sources, templates, and external content. Violations result in a zero and referral to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Reliable internet access, an LMS account (provided by the institution), and basic digital content creation tools (screen recording, image editing).
